category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Similar Questions/Part B/Abstract Algebra — Ring Theory Ideals and PIDs

questiontext.text: A ring \((R,+,\cdot)\) is a set with two operations where \((R,+)\) is an abelian group and \(\cdot\) satisfies:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) Associativity and distributivity over addition

A ring requires: (1) \((R,+)\) is an abelian group, (2) multiplication is associative: \((ab)c=a(bc)\), (3) distributivity: \(a(b+c)=ab+ac\) and \((a+b)c=ac+bc\). Commutativity of multiplication is NOT required (that's a commutative ring). Multiplicative identity is optional (ring with unity if it exists).

answer.text: Being an abelian group under multiplication too

questiontext.text: A non-empty subset \(I\) of a commutative ring \(R\) is an ideal if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(I\) is a subgroup under addition and \(rI\subseteq I\) for all \(r\in R\)

An ideal is an additive subgroup closed under multiplication by ring elements: \(a,b\in I\Rightarrow a-b\in I\), and \(r\in R,a\in I\Rightarrow ra\in I\). In a commutative ring, left = right = two-sided ideal. Ideals are kernels of ring homomorphisms, analogous to normal subgroups.

answer.text: \(I\) is an additive subgroup and \(rI\subseteq I\) for all \(r\in R\)

questiontext.text: An ideal \(P\) in a commutative ring \(R\) is prime if and only if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(ab\in P\Rightarrow a\in P\) or \(b\in P\)

Prime ideal: \(P\neq R\) and \(ab\in P\Rightarrow a\in P\) or \(b\in P\). Equivalently, \(R/P\) is an integral domain. In \(\mathbb{Z}\): \((p)\) is prime iff \(p\) is prime (or 0). Every maximal ideal is prime (in a commutative ring with unity), but not every prime is maximal (e.g., \((0)\) in \(\mathbb{Z}\)).

answer.text: \(P\) is generated by a prime number

questiontext.text: An ideal \(M\) in a commutative ring \(R\) with unity is maximal if and only if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(R/M\) is a field

In a commutative ring with unity: \(M\) is maximal \(\Leftrightarrow\) \(R/M\) is a field. Since every field is an integral domain, maximal ideals are prime. In \(\mathbb{Z}\): \((p)\) is maximal for prime \(p\) (since \(\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z}\cong\mathbb{F}_p\) is a field). \((0)\) is prime but not maximal in \(\mathbb{Z}\).

answer.text: \(R/M\) is an integral domain but not a field

questiontext.text: A Principal Ideal Domain (PID) is an integral domain in which:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) Every ideal is generated by a single element

PID: an integral domain where every ideal is principal (generated by one element: \(I=(a)=\{ra:r\in R\}\)). Examples: \(\mathbb{Z}\), \(F[x]\) for fields \(F\), \(\mathbb{Z}[i]\) (Gaussian integers). In a PID: irreducible = prime, and the Euclidean algorithm works (if it's also a Euclidean domain).

answer.text: Every ideal is generated by a single element
It has only finitely many ideals

name.text: Ring Theory - Z is a PID

questiontext.text: In the ring \(\mathbb{Z}\), every ideal is of the form \((n)=n\mathbb{Z}\) for some \(n\geq0\). The ideal \((6)\cap(4)\) in \(\mathbb{Z}\) equals:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \((12)\)

In \(\mathbb{Z}\): \((m)\cap(n)=(\mathrm{lcm}(m,n))\). \(\mathrm{lcm}(6,4)=12\). So \((6)\cap(4)=(12)\). Also \((m)+(n)=(\gcd(m,n))\): \((6)+(4)=(2)\). These follow from the fact that \(\mathbb{Z}\) is a PID.

questiontext.text: A commutative ring with unity is an integral domain if and only if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) It has no zero divisors

Integral domain: a commutative ring with unity and no zero divisors (\(ab=0\Rightarrow a=0\) or \(b=0\)). The cancellation law holds: \(ac=bc,c\neq0\Rightarrow a=b\). Examples: \(\mathbb{Z},\mathbb{Q},\mathbb{R},\mathbb{C},\mathbb{Z}[x]\). Non-example: \(\mathbb{Z}_6\) (since \(2\cdot3=6\equiv0\)).

answer.text: Every element has a multiplicative inverse

questiontext.text: The quotient ring \(\mathbb{Z}[x]/(x^2+1)\) is isomorphic to:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(\mathbb{C}\) (the complex numbers)

The map \(x\mapsto i\) gives a surjective ring homomorphism \(\mathbb{Z}[x]\to\mathbb{Z}[i]\), and similarly \(\mathbb{R}[x]/(x^2+1)\cong\mathbb{C}\). Since \(x^2+1\) is irreducible over \(\mathbb{R}\), \((x^2+1)\) is a maximal ideal in \(\mathbb{R}[x]\), so the quotient is a field isomorphic to \(\mathbb{C}\).

questiontext.text: A Unique Factorization Domain (UFD) is a domain where every non-zero non-unit element:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) Factors uniquely into irreducibles (up to order and units)

UFD: every non-zero non-unit element \(a\) can be written as \(a=p_1\cdots p_n\) with irreducibles \(p_i\), and this factorization is unique up to order and associates. Examples: \(\mathbb{Z}\), \(F[x]\), any PID. The hierarchy: fields \(\subset\) EDs \(\subset\) PIDs \(\subset\) UFDs \(\subset\) integral domains.

answer.text: Is a product of prime ideals
Factors uniquely into irreducibles (up to order and associates)

name.text: Ring Theory - Characteristic of a Ring

questiontext.text: The characteristic of the ring \(\mathbb{Z}_n\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(n\)

The characteristic of a ring is the smallest positive integer \(m\) such that \(m\cdot1=0\), or 0 if no such \(m\) exists. In \(\mathbb{Z}_n\): \(n\cdot1=n\equiv0\), so char\((\mathbb{Z}_n)=n\). For integral domains, the characteristic is either 0 or prime.

questiontext.text: In \(\mathbb{Z}[x]\), the ideal \((2,x)\) (generated by 2 and \(x\)) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) Maximal but not principal

\((2,x)=\{p(x)\in\mathbb{Z}[x]:p(0)\text{ is even}\}\). The quotient \(\mathbb{Z}[x]/(2,x)\cong\mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z}=\mathbb{F}_2\) (a field), so \((2,x)\) is maximal. It is not principal: if \((2,x)=(f(x))\), then \(f|2\) and \(f|x\), so \(f=\pm1\), giving \((2,x)=(1)=\mathbb{Z}[x]\), contradiction. This shows \(\mathbb{Z}[x]\) is NOT a PID.

questiontext.text: Chinese Remainder Theorem (ring form): if \(\gcd(m,n)=1\), then:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(\mathbb{Z}_{mn}\cong\mathbb{Z}_m\times\mathbb{Z}_n\)

CRT: if \(\gcd(m,n)=1\), then \(\mathbb{Z}_{mn}\cong\mathbb{Z}_m\times\mathbb{Z}_n\) as rings. More generally: if \(I+J=R\) (coprime ideals), then \(R/(I\cap J)\cong R/I\times R/J\). Used to solve simultaneous congruences \(x\equiv a\pmod m,x\equiv b\pmod n\).

questiontext.text: The units (invertible elements) in the Gaussian integers \(\mathbb{Z}[i]=\{a+bi:a,b\in\mathbb{Z}\}\) are:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(\{1,-1,i,-i\}\)

The norm \(N(a+bi)=a^2+b^2\) is multiplicative. \(u\) is a unit iff \(N(u)=1\) (since \(N(uv)=N(u)N(v)=1\) requires \(N(u)=N(v)=1\) for integers). \(N(a+bi)=1\Rightarrow a^2+b^2=1\Rightarrow(a,b)\in\{(\pm1,0),(0,\pm1)\}\), giving units \(\{1,-1,i,-i\}\).

questiontext.text: The nilradical of a commutative ring \(R\) (set of all nilpotent elements) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) The intersection of all prime ideals of \(R\)

Nilradical \(\mathfrak{N}(R)=\{a\in R:a^n=0\text{ for some }n\geq1\}=\bigcap_{\text{prime }P}P\). This is an ideal (even a radical ideal). In an integral domain, the nilradical is \(\{0\}\) (no nilpotents except 0). In \(\mathbb{Z}_4\): the nilradical is \(\{0,2\}\).

answer.text: The set of all units of \(R\)
The intersection of all prime ideals of \(R\)

questiontext.text: The quotient \(\mathbb{Q}[x]/(x^2-2)\) is isomorphic to:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2})=\{a+b\sqrt{2}:a,b\in\mathbb{Q}\}\)

\(x^2-2\) is irreducible over \(\mathbb{Q}\) (Eisenstein with \(p=2\)). So \((x^2-2)\) is a maximal ideal in \(\mathbb{Q}[x]\), and \(\mathbb{Q}[x]/(x^2-2)\) is a field. The coset of \(x\) plays the role of \(\sqrt{2}\): \((x+\sqrt{2}\mathbb{Q}[x])^2=2+\langle x^2-2\rangle=2\). So the quotient \(\cong\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2})\).

questiontext.text: A ring \(R\) is Noetherian if every ascending chain of ideals \(I_1\subseteq I_2\subseteq\cdots\) eventually stabilizes. Which is equivalent?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) Every ideal of \(R\) is finitely generated

Hilbert's basis theorem: \(R[x]\) is Noetherian iff \(R\) is Noetherian. Equivalences: (1) ACC on ideals, (2) every ideal is finitely generated, (3) every nonempty collection of ideals has a maximal element. Every PID is Noetherian (ideals are generated by one element, hence finitely).

answer.text: Every descending chain of ideals stabilizes
Every ideal of \(R\) is finitely generated

questiontext.text: In a UFD, an element \(p\) is irreducible if and only if it is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) Prime

In a UFD (or more generally a domain): prime \(\Rightarrow\) irreducible always. Irreducible \(\Rightarrow\) prime in UFDs. In general domains they may differ: in \(\mathbb{Z}[\sqrt{-5}]\), \(3\) is irreducible but not prime (\(3\mid(2+\sqrt{-5})(2-\sqrt{-5})=9\) but \(3\nmid(2\pm\sqrt{-5})\)).

answer.text: Prime (and vice versa, in a UFD)
An element that cannot be divided by any element
A generator of a maximal ideal

questiontext.text: In the direct product ring \(R\times S\), the ideals of the form \(I\times J\) (where \(I\trianglelefteq R\) and \(J\trianglelefteq S\)) are:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) All ideals of \(R\times S\)

Every ideal of \(R\times S\) is of the form \(I\times J\) where \(I\) is an ideal of \(R\) and \(J\) is an ideal of \(S\). This follows from the projection homomorphisms. In particular, prime ideals of \(R\times S\) are \(P\times S\) (where \(P\) is prime in \(R\)) or \(R\times Q\) (where \(Q\) is prime in \(S\)).

questiontext.text: The localization \(\mathbb{Z}_{(p)}=\{a/b\in\mathbb{Q}:\gcd(a,b)=1, p\nmid b\}\) is a local ring with unique maximal ideal:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(p\mathbb{Z}_{(p)}\)

In the localization at prime \(p\): \(\mathbb{Z}_{(p)}\) consists of rationals with denominator not divisible by \(p\). The non-units are elements with \(p\mid a\), i.e., multiples of \(p\): the unique maximal ideal is \(p\mathbb{Z}_{(p)}\). The quotient \(\mathbb{Z}_{(p)}/p\mathbb{Z}_{(p)}\cong\mathbb{F}_p\).

questiontext.text: In a commutative ring \(R\) with unity, if every ideal is prime, then \(R\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) A field

If every ideal is prime, in particular \(\{0\}\) is prime (so \(R\) is an integral domain), and every proper ideal is prime. In an integral domain, the only ideals are \(\{0\}\) and \(R\) (by hypothesis both prime), meaning \(R\) is a field (every nonzero element is a unit, since any nonzero element generates a prime, hence maximal, ideal).

answer.text: A UFD but not necessarily a PID
An integral domain but not a field
